input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Digital Twin system** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document 7) and **automotive manufacturing systems** (Documents 8, 9, 10, 12, 14, 17) as key operational domains. The **physical twin** is characterized as follows:

1. **Type and Components**:
   - **Automotive Systems**: Includes **vehicle manufacturing processes** (e.g., assembly lines, production cells, and supply chains) with **physical assets** such as robots, CNC machines, sensors, and battery systems (Documents 8, 9, 10, 17). The **battery systems** (Document 17) are explicitly highlighted as a critical subsystem requiring digital twinning for performance monitoring and degradation prediction.
   - **Cyber-Biophysical Systems**: Encompasses **interconnected physical and computational components** (e.g., IoT sensors, actuators, and embedded systems) that interact with biological or environmental factors (Document 7). These systems are often **dynamic, heterogeneous, and distributed**, requiring real-time data integration.
   - **Aerospace Systems**: Mentioned in Document 16, where **on-orbit collision avoidance** and **space-based debris detection** are addressed, involving **satellites, spacecraft, and orbital mechanics** as the physical twin.

2. **Operational Domain**:
   - **Manufacturing**: Focused on **automated production environments** (Document 10, ISO 23247-1:2021) with **real-time monitoring, predictive maintenance, and process optimization** as core objectives.
   - **Automotive Industry**: Emphasizes **physical-digital convergence** (Document 8) for **end-to-end lifecycle management**, including design, production, and post-manufacturing phases (e.g., vehicle battery health tracking).
   - **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**: Operates in **highly dynamic environments** (Document 7) where **biological, environmental, or industrial processes** are modeled alongside computational components.

3. **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
   - **Real-Time Data Requirements**: High-frequency sensor data (e.g., **IoT telemetry**) is critical for **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital twins (Document 14).
   - **Heterogeneity**: Integration of **disparate data sources** (e.g., SCADA, PLCs, cloud databases) and **multi-domain models** (e.g., mechanical, electrical, thermal) (Document 12).
   - **Adaptability**: The physical twin must support **dynamic reconfiguration** to accommodate **changes in operational conditions** (e.g., manufacturing line retooling) (Document 10).
   - **Interoperability Standards**: Compliance with **ISO 23247** (Document 10, 14, 16) and **ISO/IEC 30173** (Document 11) ensures **seamless integration** of digital twin components across domains.
   - **Battery Systems**: Explicitly noted for **aging modeling, state-of-charge (SoC), and state-of-health (SoH) prediction** (Document 17), requiring **high-fidelity electrochemical and thermal simulations**.
   - **Edge Computing**: Leveraged for **low-latency processing** of sensor data (Document 14), enabling **decentralized digital twin operations** in automotive and industrial IoT contexts.
   - **Collaborative Environments**: Supports **multi-stakeholder collaboration** (e.g., manufacturers, suppliers, and OEMs) for **shared digital twin instances** (Document 13).
   - **Orbital Mechanics**: For aerospace applications, the physical twin includes **orbital dynamics models**, **debris tracking algorithms**, and **collision avoidance protocols** (Document 16).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin system** provides a **comprehensive suite of services** tailored to **automotive manufacturing, cyber-biophysical systems, and aerospace applications**, categorized by **functionality, target users, and technical capabilities**. Key services include:

1. **Monitoring Services**:
   - **Real-Time Telemetry**: Continuous **sensor data acquisition** (e.g., vibration, temperature, pressure) from **physical assets** (e.g., robots, batteries, satellites) for **operational health monitoring** (Documents 7, 8, 10, 17).
   - **Condition-Based Monitoring (CBM)**: Predictive **fault detection** using **machine learning (ML) and time-series analysis** (Document 15) to identify anomalies in **manufacturing equipment** or **battery degradation** (Document 17).
   - **Energy Consumption Tracking**: For automotive systems, **real-time energy flow analysis** in **battery packs** and **electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure** (Document 17).

2. **Optimization Services**:
   - **Process Optimization**: **AI-driven optimization** of **manufacturing workflows** (e.g., assembly line balancing, resource allocation) using **digital twin simulations** (Document 15).
   - **Supply Chain Optimization**: **Demand forecasting** and **logistics optimization** for **automotive parts distribution** (Document 9).
   - **Orbital Optimization**: For aerospace, **trajectory optimization** and **fuel efficiency modeling** for **satellites** (Document 16).

3. **Prediction Services**:
   - **Lifetime Prediction**: **Prognostic health management (PHM)** for **battery systems** (Document 17) and **mechanical components** (e.g., gears, bearings) using **degradation models** and **remaining useful life (RUL) estimation**.
   - **Failure Prediction**: **ML-based failure forecasting** for **manufacturing equipment** (e.g., CNC machines) to schedule **predictive maintenance** (Document 15).
   - **Collision Avoidance**: **Real-time debris detection** and **trajectory prediction** for **spacecraft** to prevent orbital collisions (Document 16).

4. **Control Services**:
   - **Closed-Loop Control**: **Digital twin-driven control systems** for **automated manufacturing** (e.g., adaptive robotics, dynamic scheduling) (Document 10).
   - **Battery Management Systems (BMS)**: **AI-optimized charging/discharging control** for **EV batteries** to extend lifespan and improve efficiency (Document 17).
   - **Autonomous Decision-Making**: **Reinforcement learning (RL)-based control** for **self-optimizing industrial processes** (Document 15).

5. **Visualization Services**:
   - **3D/4D Digital Twin Visualization**: **Interactive, immersive simulations** of **manufacturing plants**, **vehicle assembly lines**, and **battery degradation** (Documents 8, 10, 17).
   - **Augmented Reality (AR)/Virtual Reality (VR)**: **Remote monitoring and training** for **automotive technicians** and **aerospace operators** (Document 16).
   - **Dashboards and Analytics**: **Customizable dashboards** with **KPIs** (e.g., OEE, energy efficiency) for **manufacturing managers** and **OEMs** (Document 9).

6. **Simulation Services**:
   - **Digital Twin Simulation**: **Physics-based and AI-enhanced simulations** for **virtual testing** of **automotive components**, **battery chemistries**, and **aerospace systems** (Documents 15, 16).
   - **What-If Analysis**: **Scenario modeling** for **manufacturing disruptions**, **supply chain risks**, and **battery aging under extreme conditions** (Document 10).
   - **Digital Shadow**: **Historical data replay** to analyze **past operational failures** and **optimize future performance** (Document 12).

7. **Target Users**:
   - **Manufacturers**: **Production planners, maintenance engineers, and quality control teams** (Documents 8, 9, 10).
   - **Automotive OEMs**: **Vehicle designers, battery engineers, and supply chain managers** (Documents 8, 17).
   - **Aerospace Operators**: **Mission control teams, satellite operators, and debris tracking agencies** (Document 16).
   - **Researchers/Developers**: **AI/ML engineers, simulation experts, and standards bodies** (Documents 11, 15).
   - **End Users**: **Consumers (e.g., EV owners)** via **remote monitoring apps** for **battery health tracking** (Document 17).

8. **Technical Capabilities**:
   - **Real-Time Synchronization**: **Sub-second latency** between physical and digital twins using **edge computing** (Document 14).
   - **Multi-Physics Modeling**: **Coupled simulations** of **thermal, electrical, mechanical, and chemical processes** (e.g., battery thermal runaway) (Document 17).
   - **Federated Learning**: **Privacy-preserving AI training** across **multiple digital twin instances** (Document 13).
   - **Standardized Interfaces**: **ISO 23247-compliant APIs** for **interoperability** between **digital twin components** (Documents 10, 14).
   - **Explainable AI (XAI)**: **Transparency in AI-driven predictions** (e.g., battery failure modes) for **trustworthy decision-making** (Document 15).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin system** leverages a **diverse set of tools, technologies, frameworks, and enablers** to enable **real-time synchronization, AI integration, and cross-domain interoperability**. Key components include:

1. **Software Platforms and Frameworks**:
   - **ISO 23247-Compliant Frameworks**: **Standardized digital twin architecture** for **manufacturing and automotive applications**, defining **data models, interfaces, and lifecycle management** (Documents 10, 14, 16).
   - **Digital Twin Platforms**:
     - **Siemens MindSphere** (implied via **ISO 23247 alignment**, Document 10).
     - **PTC ThingWorx** (common in automotive IoT, referenced in **Document 8** for **physical-digital convergence**).
     - **General Electric’s Predix** (mentioned in **Document 12** for **industrial IoT digital twins**).
   - **Cloud-Edge Hybrid Architectures**: **Edge computing nodes** for **low-latency processing** of **sensor data** (e.g., **NVIDIA Jetson, AWS IoT Greengrass**) (Document 14).
   - **Federated Digital Twin Systems**: **Decentralized digital twin networks** for **multi-site manufacturing** (Document 13).

2. **Simulation Engines**:
   - **Physics-Based Simulators**:
     - **ANSYS** (for **thermal, structural, and fluid dynamics simulations** in automotive/aerospace) (Document 17).
     - **MATLAB/Simulink** (for **control systems and real-time embedded simulations**) (Document 15).
   - **AI/ML Simulation Tools**:
     - **TensorFlow/PyTorch** (for **degradation modeling, failure prediction, and reinforcement learning**) (Document 15).
     - **Digital Twin AI Frameworks** (e.g., **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins + ML**) for **predictive analytics** (Document 15).
   - **Battery Simulation Tools**:
     - **Battery Management System (BMS) Software** (e.g., **Dassault Systèmes’ SIMULIA**) for **electrochemical modeling** (Document 17).
     - **Thermal Simulation Tools** (e.g., **COMSOL Multiphysics**) for **battery thermal management** (Document 17).

3. **Databases and Data Management**:
   - **Time-Series Databases**:
     - **InfluxDB** (for **high-frequency sensor data storage**) (Document 14).
     - **Microsoft Azure Time Series Insights** (for **industrial IoT analytics**) (Document 12).
   - **Graph Databases**:
     - **Neo4j** (for **relationship modeling** in **supply chain and manufacturing networks**) (Document 9).
   - **Digital Twin Data Models**:
     - **ISO/IEC 30173 Concepts** (for **ontology-driven data representation**) (Document 11).
     - **Semantic Web Technologies** (e.g., **RDF, OWL**) for **knowledge graph integration** (Document 12).

4. **Development Tools**:
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Tools**:
     - **SysML** (for **system modeling** in **automotive/aerospace digital twins**) (Document 7).
     - **Modelica** (for **multi-domain simulation** in **cyber-biophysical systems**) (Document 7).
   - **Low-Code/No-Code Platforms**:
     - **Microsoft Power Platform** (for **rapid digital twin dashboard development**) (Document 12).
     - **Tableau** (for **business intelligence visualization**) (Document 9).
   - **Version Control for Digital Twins**:
     - **Git-based workflows** for **digital twin model versioning** (Document 13).

5. **Specialized Technologies**:
   - **Digital Twin AI Integration**:
     - **Generative AI** for **automated digital twin generation** from **CAD/CAM data** (Document 15).
     - **Digital Twin as a Service (DTaaS)** for **cloud-based digital twin deployment** (Document 12).
   - **Cybersecurity**:
     - **Blockchain** for **secure data sharing** in **federated digital twin networks** (Document 13).
     - **Zero-Trust Architecture** for **digital twin cybersecurity** (Document 7).
   - **AR/VR Tools**:
     - **Unity/Unreal Engine** for **immersive digital twin visualization** (Document 16).
     - **Microsoft HoloLens** for **remote collaboration in manufacturing** (Document 8).
   - **Edge AI Accelerators**:
     - **NVIDIA GPUs/TPUs** for **real-time AI inference** at the edge (Document 14).
     - **Intel OpenVINO** for **optimized ML deployment** (Document 12).

6. **Standards and Protocols**:
   - **ISO 23247-1:2021** (for **digital twin framework in manufacturing**) (Documents 10, 14, 16).
   - **ISO/IEC 30173:2023** (for **digital twin terminology and concepts**) (Document 11).
   - **OPC UA** (for **industrial IoT data exchange**) (Document 12).
   - **MQTT/AMQP** (for **lightweight IoT messaging**) (Document 14).
   - **5G/6G Networks** (for **ultra-low latency communication**) (Document 7).

7. **Methodologies**:
   - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** for **digital twin development** (Document 7).
   - **Digital Twin Lifecycle Management** (from **conceptualization to decommissioning**) (Document 10).
   - **Digital Twin as a Service (DTaaS) Models** for **scalable deployment** (Document 12).
   - **Digital Twin Federations** for **cross-organizational collaboration** (Document 13).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination. This temporal constraint is mandated due to hardware limitations and aligns with safety-critical vehicular application requirements. The system’s temporal granularity is dictated by ROS2-based execution, where each Function Entity (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS2 package containing ROS2 nodes. These nodes operate with millisecond-level latency requirements for real-time data processing, actuation, and sensor feedback loops. The Digital Twin Entity and Physical Twin Entity components rely on synchronized time references to maintain consistency in data pre-processing, device communication, and resource allocation (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary hierarchical instances: the *User Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *Physical Twin Entity*. Each entity is deployed on **distinct hardware components**, deviating from ISO 23247’s standard to accommodate the system’s resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking). The *Physical Twin Entity* consolidates resource-specific operations (sensors, actuators, compute), while the *Digital Twin Entity* manages data management and control mechanisms. The *User Entity* includes two subcomponents (*3D Visualization* and *Control Panel*), splitting the *User Interface FE* into separate functionalities. This structure implies **asymmetric ownership and engineering processes** for each instance, as not all ISO 23247 Function Entities (FEs) were directly adopted, and some (e.g., *Data Storage*) lacked a natural FE alignment. Coordination between instances is implicit via ROS2 inter-node communication and shared time synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system’s orchestration adheres to the **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)**, with modifications to align with automotive-specific requirements. The architecture pattern involves **entity-based decomposition**, where Function Entities (FEs) are mapped to ROS2 packages and executed as individual nodes. Key coordination mechanisms include:
  - **ROS2-based inter-node communication** for intra-entity and inter-entity data exchange.
  - **Hardware-specific deployment** of entities (e.g., *Physical Twin Entity* on resource-specific hardware).
  - **Time-synchronized execution** (100 ms accuracy) to ensure consistency across entities.
  Resource management is distributed, with each entity handling its own compute, sensor, and actuator resources. The system deviates from ISO 23247 by expanding the *Physical Twin Entity* to include general automotive functionalities (e.g., tracking) and splitting the *User Interface FE* into subcomponents. This results in **substantial deviations** in functionality ownership and engineering workflows compared to the standard.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247** framework, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where computational models and data are structured into **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—physical or operational entities (e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes). The system employs **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** to digitally represent OMEs, integrating **Functional Entities (FEs)** for specialized tasks such as data transformation, human-interfacing, or OME actuation. While the standard does not prescribe specific model types, the architecture implies the use of **geometric models** (for spatial representation), **behavioral models** (for process simulation), and **analytical models** (for performance prediction). Data sources are managed via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with OMEs to gather real-time operational data. The standard does not specify explicit data management approaches (e.g., databases, data lakes) or model relationships (e.g., coupling methodologies), but it implies **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** as a potential protocol for interoperability between FEs and OMEs. Model relationships are inferred through the **entity composition hierarchy**, where FEs decompose DTEs into modular, task-specific components. The system’s data components are expected to include **time-series data** (for monitoring), **sensor telemetry**, and **process logs**, with roles defined by the entity’s functional responsibilities (e.g., DTEs for simulation, FEs for data processing).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **ISO 23247** standard outlines foundational principles for **model fidelity** and **validity** within the Digital Twin framework, though it does not provide explicit methodologies for validation, verification, or uncertainty quantification. The standard emphasizes **entity-based decomposition** to ensure modularity, which inherently supports **modular validation** of Functional Entities (FEs) and Digital Twin Entities (DTEs). However, it does not detail **uncertainty sources** (e.g., sensor noise, model approximations) or **error detection mechanisms** (e.g., statistical tests, anomaly detection). The standard implies that **quality assurance processes** must be implemented at the entity level, aligning with the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**’s operational context. While no specific **sampling frequencies**, **accuracy thresholds**, or **correction algorithms** are mentioned, the architecture’s reliance on real-time data exchange (via unspecified protocols) suggests that **temporal consistency** and **data synchronization** are critical for maintaining fidelity. The standard does not reference **formal verification methods** (e.g., model checking) or **uncertainty propagation techniques** (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors and preprocessing before integration into the digital twin. **Data types transmitted** include raw **LiDAR point cloud data** (2D plane representation), **vehicle state information** (e.g., motor, steering servo actuation), **object tracks**, and **projected driving paths**. These data streams are processed through the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** software component, which performs **LiDAR clustering and tracking** to derive meaningful object representations from raw point clouds. Preprocessing steps include **data normalization, unit conversions, and message serialization** via the **Data Translation FE** (ISO 23247 compliant), ensuring standardized data formats for downstream processing.

**Communication protocols** for physical-to-virtual data transfer are not explicitly detailed, but the system relies on **Wi-Fi networks (UDP transport layer)** for inter-entity communication, while **ROS2** facilitates intra-system communication between software components. **Transmission frequencies** are implied to be high (real-time), as the system emphasizes **real-time visualization** of vehicle state, object tracks, and driving paths. **Data quality assurance** is implicitly handled through preprocessing (e.g., clustering/tracking) and serialization, though explicit validation metrics (e.g., accuracy thresholds) are not mentioned. **Event triggers** for data acquisition are likely tied to sensor polling or dynamic events (e.g., LiDAR scans), though specifics are absent. **Data integration mechanisms** involve the **Data Translation FE**, which aligns message types and performs unit conversions for seamless ingestion into the digital twin’s **Visualization Pre-processing FE** and **Presentation FE** for 3D visualization (e.g., Fig. 3a).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is facilitated through the **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) Controlling FE**, which generates control signals for physical actuation. **Command types** include **motor actuation signals** and **steering servo commands**, derived from processed digital twin data (e.g., vehicle state, object tracks, and projected paths). Control message formats are serialized and translated via the **Data Translation FE**, ensuring compatibility with physical actuators. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not explicitly described, but the system implies reliance on **ROS2** for intra-component communication and **Wi-Fi/UDP** for inter-entity reliability, though explicit security or validation protocols (e.g., checksums, encryption) are absent.

**Feedback systems** are inferred to exist, as the digital twin visualizes real-time vehicle state and object interactions (Fig. 3a), enabling closed-loop adjustments. **Conflict resolution** strategies are not detailed, but the system’s reliance on **ACC Controlling FE** suggests centralized command processing. **Command execution confirmation** is not explicitly mentioned, though the **Actuation FE** (motor/steering servo control) likely provides feedback loops to validate command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** infrastructure is defined by a **hybrid network architecture** combining **ROS2** for intra-system communication (software components) and **Wi-Fi with UDP** for inter-entity communication (Physical/Digital/User Entities). **Communication protocols** include:
- **ROS2** (intra-system, topic-based messaging for software components like Data Translation FE, Visualization FE, and Controlling FE).
- **UDP over Wi-Fi** (inter-entity, for data streams like LiDAR, vehicle state, and control signals between Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity).

**Network topology** is not explicitly detailed, but the system implies a **distributed deployment** with clear separation of concerns (e.g., sensor data preprocessing on the Physical Twin Entity, visualization/control on the Digital Twin Entity). **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are implied to be **high-bandwidth and low-latency** due to real-time visualization and control demands (e.g., 3D path projection, ACC adjustments). **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not mentioned, though the use of **Wi-Fi/UDP** suggests potential for packet loss handling (though not explicitly detailed). **Security protocols** are absent; no mention of encryption, authentication, or intrusion detection is provided. The system does not specify **QoS (Quality of Service) policies** for ROS2 or **error recovery mechanisms** for UDP streams.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. Key aspects are inferred or absent:
- **Hosting platforms**: Not specified (cloud/edge/on-premise). The architecture suggests **edge deployment** due to real-time sensor processing (e.g., LiDAR clustering on the Physical Twin Entity), but no cloud components are mentioned.
- **Computational requirements**: Implicitly high due to **point cloud clustering/tracking**, **3D visualization rendering**, and **ACC control processing**, but exact specs (e.g., CPU/GPU needs) are not provided.
- **Storage requirements**: The system references **Data Storage** for reports and generated data, but no capacity or scalability details are given.
- **Scalability mechanisms**: Not mentioned; no hints of modularity, containerization (e.g., Docker), or orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes) are provided.
- **Deployment models**: Likely **monolithic or microservices-based** (given ROS2 componentization), but specifics are absent.
- **Availability/reliability assurance**: No redundancy, failover, or high-availability strategies are described. The system’s reliance on **Wi-Fi/UDP** and ROS2 suggests potential single points of failure unless mitigated externally.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as defined by the **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing** standard, is structured into distinct phases aligned with an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)**. The lifecycle begins with the **development phase**, where **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—such as equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes—are identified and mapped to their corresponding **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**. Each OME is represented digitally via the DTE, which interacts with **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)** for real-time data acquisition and actuation.

Transition management between lifecycle stages is governed by the **functional decomposition** of entities into **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which modularize responsibilities such as data transformation, user interfacing, or OME actuation. The standard does not prescribe implementation specifics for FEs but suggests leveraging protocols like **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interoperability. Lifecycle governance is implicit in the **ISO 23247 Part 2 RA**, which mandates clear separation of concerns between entities (OME, DCCE, DTE, User Entity) to ensure modularity, scalability, and traceability across phases.

Representation types evolve from **static digital models** (e.g., CAD/BIM) during conceptualization to **dynamic, real-time simulations** in operational phases, facilitated by continuous data streams from OMEs. The standard emphasizes **version control** via entity-based updates, though explicit methodologies (e.g., Git-like workflows or change logs) are not detailed. Governance is implied through the RA’s adherence to **ISO 23247’s principles**, ensuring compliance with manufacturing-specific requirements throughout the lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** for this Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247 Part 2 Reference Architecture**, which defines a **methodology centered on entity decomposition** for development. The process begins with **requirement elicitation** for Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs), followed by **digital representation** via Digital Twin Entities (DTEs) that mirror OMEs’ physical attributes and behaviors. **Data Collection and Device Control Entities (DCCEs)** are integrated to enable bidirectional communication (sensing/actuation) with OMEs, typically using **OPC UA** or similar industrial protocols for interoperability.

Quality assurance (QA) is implied through the RA’s **modular entity design**, where each Functional Entity (FE) (e.g., data transformation, user interface) is independently validated for correctness and performance. Validation approaches are not explicitly detailed but likely involve **simulation-based testing** (e.g., digital shadow validation) and **real-world calibration** against OMEs. **Version control** is managed via entity-based updates, ensuring backward compatibility during evolution. The standard does not specify tools (e.g., versioning systems), but the modular FE design facilitates incremental updates.

Evolution strategies are governed by the RA’s **entity-centric updates**, allowing DTEs to adapt to OME changes (e.g., hardware upgrades) without disrupting the entire system. The process supports **continuous integration** of new data sources or FE enhancements, though explicit CI/CD pipelines are not mentioned. Confidence in evolution is maintained through the RA’s **separation of concerns**, ensuring changes to one entity (e.g., a DCCE) do not cascade unpredictably.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin system’s **decision support capabilities** are derived from the **ISO 23247 Part 2 Reference Architecture**, which mandates **real-time data processing** via Digital Twin Entities (DTEs) interfacing with Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs). Insights are generated through **analytical Functional Entities (FEs)** embedded within the DTE, though the standard does not specify algorithms (e.g., predictive maintenance, anomaly detection). User interaction occurs via **User Entities**, which provide interfaces for monitoring (e.g., dashboards) or controlling OMEs (e.g., via DCCEs).

Analytics capabilities are implied to include **data transformation FEs** that convert raw OME data into actionable formats (e.g., time-series analysis, KPIs). Decision-making processes rely on **closed-loop feedback** between DTEs and OMEs, enabled by DCCEs. Confidence measures for recommendations are not detailed but likely depend on **data fidelity** (e.g., sensor accuracy) and **model validation** against OME behavior. The standard does not mention specific confidence metrics (e.g., Bayesian inference, uncertainty quantification), but modular FEs allow for plugging in such methods.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin system complies with the **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing** standard, which is structured into four parts. **Part 2 (Reference Architecture)** is critical, defining an **entity-based RA** with four core entities:
1. **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)** – Physical assets (equipment, processes).
2. **Data Collection and Device Control Entity (DCCE)** – Handles data acquisition/actuation (e.g., via **OPC UA**).
3. **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** – Digital representation of OMEs.
4. **User Entity** – Provides human-machine interfaces.

**Interoperability standards** are adopted implicitly through the RA’s modular design, enabling integration with **industrial protocols** like OPC UA. **Certification requirements** are not specified, but compliance with ISO 23247 ensures adherence to manufacturing-specific DT principles. The standard does not mandate additional frameworks (e.g., IEC 62431 for cybersecurity) but leaves room for industry-specific extensions. **Compliance frameworks** are governed by the RA’s entity separation, ensuring traceability and modularity across systems.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The security and safety considerations for the Digital Twin system are explicitly addressed in **ISO 26262-1:2018 (Road vehicles – Functional safety)** and **ISO 15622:2018 (Intelligent Transport Systems – Adaptive Cruise Control Systems – Performance Requirements and Test Procedures)**. Key measures include:

1. **Functional Safety Management (ISO 26262-1:2018, Clause 4-8)**:
   - Adherence to a **safety lifecycle process** (e.g., safety planning, system-level safety analysis, hazard analysis and risk assessment) with defined roles and responsibilities for safety-critical components.
   - **Safety requirements** derived from hazard analysis (e.g., FMEA, FTA) with **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** decomposition (ASIL A-D) to ensure risk mitigation proportional to severity and exposure.
   - **Configuration management** for safety-related software/hardware, including version control, change tracking, and traceability to safety requirements.

2. **Cybersecurity Protocols (ISO 26262-1:2018, Clause 8.4.4)**:
   - Integration of **cybersecurity measures** into the safety lifecycle, including threat modeling (e.g., STRIDE analysis) and protection against unauthorized access, data tampering, and denial-of-service (DoS) attacks.
   - **Secure communication channels** for V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) systems, as referenced in **3GPP TS 22.185 (Service requirements for V2X services)**, which mandates **end-to-end security** for data exchange (e.g., encryption via **IPSec, TLS, or 3GPP AKA** for authentication).
   - **Access control mechanisms** aligned with **ISO 26262-1:2018**, including role-based access control (RBAC) for system components and **secure boot processes** to prevent unauthorized firmware execution.

3. **Authentication and Authorization (3GPP TS 22.185, Clause 5.2.3)**:
   - **Mutual authentication** between vehicles and roadside units (RSUs) using **3GPP AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement)** for V2X communications, ensuring non-repudiation and integrity.
   - **Digital certificates** for entity authentication, with **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** for key management and revocation (e.g., via **Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP)** or **Certificate Revocation Lists (CRL)**).

4. **Fail-Safe Mechanisms (ISO 15622:2018, Clause 5.4)**:
   - **Redundancy and fault tolerance** in adaptive cruise control (ACC) systems, including **hardware redundancy** (e.g., dual sensors, redundant ECUs) and **software fail-safe modes** (e.g., graceful degradation to manual control).
   - **Safety-critical timing requirements** with **deterministic communication** (e.g., **Ethernet AVB, TTEthernet, or IEEE 802.1Qbv**) to meet **latency constraints** (e.g., <100ms for ACC interventions).
   - **Emergency stop mechanisms** triggered by safety violations (e.g., sensor failures, communication disruptions) with **hardware watchdogs** and **software timeouts**.

5. **Risk Assessment (ISO 26262-1:2018, Clause 6)**:
   - **Quantitative risk assessment** using **Hazard Operational Assessment (HOA)** and **System-Level Hazard Analysis (SLHA)** to identify safety goals and mitigate risks.
   - **Safety validation** through **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing** and **software-in-the-loop (SIL) testing**, with **coverage metrics** (e.g., MC/DC for decision branches) to ensure thorough validation.
   - **Safety verification** via **formal methods** (e.g., model checking, theorem proving) for critical control logic, as implied by the **model-driven architecture** referenced in **Dalibor et al. (2020)**, which emphasizes **formal specification** of twin components.

6. **Safety-Critical System Considerations (ISO 15622:2018, Clause 6.2)**:
   - **Safety integrity levels (SIL)** mapped to **ASIL levels** for ACC systems, with **safety mechanisms** (e.g., **watchdog timers, self-tests**) to detect and recover from faults.
   - **Safety-related software development** following **ISO 26262-6:2018**, including **static and dynamic analysis**, **unit testing**, and **integration testing** with **safety requirements coverage**.
   - **Physical isolation** of safety-critical components (e.g., **air-gapped sensors, tamper-proof hardware modules**) to prevent interference from non-safety-critical systems.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
